author: Gwailow
msg:
#ama Before we go for yet another vote - can we get some clarity from the Team on what legal review has been completed on the concept of UNOs voting to set a minimum revenue share in the marketplace? 

The more I consider it, the more concerned I am that any such move could be deemed illegal in UK/US/EU under anti-competition/antitrust law. I am only familiar with the detail of the UK version (EU is very similar) but there is an argument that collusion and agreement between competitors (UNOs in this case) to fix the price (the ULO share) and provide the ULO with less choice or power to make independent decisions on which licenses to lease, would be in breach of the law. Collusion would be pretty easy to prove here given the 1000s of TG messages debating what level the price should be fixed at. The price in this case would be the financial arrangement of the ULO revenue share (unclear if this is an issue - sharing UPs only but UPs are redeemable for dollars).

I don't want to be a killjoy here but before we go any further, can we get some confirmation that this has been considered and cleared by the team's legal advisors?

author: Calus B
msg: I think because we are not limiting but an individual UNO ™ does it doesn’t get covered. Any uno can give any split. It’s only on the default marketplace that a minimum is set

author: Coffee Nodes
msg: also customers don't pay, they just earn less. So it's not "price fixing"

author: Gwailow
msg: "It’s only on the default marketplace that a minimum is set" - this is where my concern lies. The default public marketplace is potentially limiting customer choice and setting standard terms.

author: Gwailow
msg: im pretty sure the financial terms of the lease agreement would get caught in the definition of 'price'. Maybe not given that we're only sharing UPs. But would prefer that this is determined through a legal team rather than us all guessing.

author: Mat
msg:
@defiants_kyle Can this question please be added to the backlog of questions for the team and/or be added to #amaquestions:

As a UNO, my core responsibilities is finding ULO's & keeping them engaged and active on the network, it's their participation that is essential for generating revenue across the network.

The concern I have is how clearly UNOs will be informed about the actual production activation date of each task, not just when the task appears in the app or becomes visible to clients, but when it is genuinely live from a ULO’s perspective.

We’ve just seen this issue with the “Caller ID Testing” task. It was “launched” and appeared in the Unity app four days ago, yet it isn’t actually receiving call requests from clients. I communicated this task to my ULOs at launch, and now they’re asking why no calls are coming through. Without clarity on activation timing, ULOs are getting frustrated, trust in the project weakens, and engagement becomes harder for all of us; the UNOs, the team, and the wider network.

**Question**: For future task releases, will UNOs be given a clear production-activation date, the date when the task will actually begin generating work for ULOs, so we can engage and inform our communities accurately and maintain confidence and help them remain active and engaged in Unity?

Putting a 60/40 forced split delay the boarding of people that discover the app from social media and decide to get a license by themselves. Let's see when we will see $0.83/lic/day

author: Quoia75
msg:
Your interest in this topic is appreciated. Considering UNOs are being potentially cast as "franchisees" you might find the following materials informative. Skip the AI scrapebots (Grok, ChatGPT, etc) and go directly to these sources:

- (USA) FTC Franchise Rule – Statement of Basis and Purpose / Official Interpretive Guidance
- (USA) Sherman Antitrust Act, 15 U.S.C. §§ 1–2
- (US) Leegin Creative Leather Products, Inc. v. PSKS, Inc., 551 U.S. 877 (2007)
- (UK) Competition Act 1998
- (UK) Enterprise Act 2002
- (UK) UK Vertical Agreements Block Exemption Order 2022 (VABEO)
- (UK) CMA Guidance on vertical agreements and the Chapter I prohibition

If there is a pre-dominance of US or Western case law in these examples, it's simply because these countries (the US in particular) tends to have an oversized influence on how pricing law is written global.

Is it illegal to set an RPM price for a product? It depends on country and jurisdiction.

Of course, nothing above is legal advice and is provided purely for education purposes.

If you want to place this source information in a AI bot for further study I'd recommend Gemini or Perplexity over Grok and make sure to keep the bot from hallucinating or inferring by adding the following prompt to your query:

"**CAUTION: Do not infer. Only base your response on existing case law and primary legal sources**."

HTH👌

author: AK J
msg: The invisible hand / free market approach makes sense if rewards continue to rise. Who knows if that will be the case.  I would assume if rewards rise, there is more demand. With more demand, all UNOs would consider increasing their split. But like in any free market, I guarantee you will have UNOs that will compete on split and reduce the total potential market for all us. Why not set a floor?

author: Hello Pal (Connected Earth)
msg:
Sorry to bring this topic back. I have been loosely following the debate on the split between UNO and ULO. I was for one towards the lower UNO and higher ULO split. My licenses are at 30:70 at this moment. But I do understand the other side of the argument. Unfortunately, with the current 60:40 consensus, the marketplace is practically dead. 

I think there is a middle ground we can rally behind with a small tweak. 

Rule:
60:40 split for the first $10 per license / month. Thereafter, UNO will be allowed to set their own split.

$10 number can be $15 or $20. I have just put some arbitrary number to convey the idea.

Benefits:
UNOs advocating higher split: They are still incentivized to do their own marketing.

UNOs advocating lower split: This group may not have time to do their own marketing. They can still rely on marketplace.

ULO: They are incentivized to cross that $10 to get into higher tier bracket. 

I really hope community gives it a thought and decide on this common sense approach.

author: Compost Man Unity code: XY
msg: I think the issue might be that the market from unity's perspective, ie How many licences they can mint due to demand, is not the same market that the unos are in because we are competing with each other for distribution of those licences

author: Kay | Will never DM you first
msg:
From AMAs:

When a license is leased, a digital contract is created specifying the agreed percentage and duration. Once that lease period expires, you regain full rights over the license and you may choose to reclaim it or take other actions as you see fit.

author: Quoia75
msg: ...Except for subcontractors usually perform a task for the contracting party behind the scene completely under the hiring contractor's umbrella. For example, Built Well, LLC, a construction company, needs to put up a house for a client. Instead of keeping all labor in house, they pull together a pool of short-term subs to get the job done, but it's all being done under Built Well, LLC's brand name. The client will only ever see Built Well on all invoices and billing statements.

author: Quoia75
msg: With Unity you will have thousands of UNOs, some sticking strictly to using the Unity brand, others creating their own branding while distributing Unity product under their own labels. Unity has charged UNOs with marketing Unity and we have not been given any strict marching orders about how to go about that or been required to sign Teaming Agreements or Non-Disclosure Agreements that legally explain how we are suppose to conduct ourselves while representing the Unity brand as we market.

author: Quoia75
msg: For tax purposes, none of us have been required to sign any documents that state we are sub-contractors (W-9 status in the US.)
